title: Stunting vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: [Stunting](#dod:stunting) measures the share of children younger than 5 years that are too short for their age. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Stunting vs. GDP per capita, 2024

Stunting measures the share of children younger than 5 years that are too short for their age. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between national wealth and child growth outcomes, plotting the share of children under five who are stunted on the vertical axis against GDP per capita on the horizontal axis. The horizontal axis uses a logarithmic scale ranging from $2,000 to $100,000 in international dollars, while the vertical axis measures stunting prevalence from 0% to over 50%. Points are color-coded by region: North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. The data shows a clear negative relationship where higher GDP per capita coincides with lower stunting rates. At the lower end of the income scale, countries like Burundi, Niger, and the Democratic Republic of Congo cluster in the upper-left quadrant with stunting rates between 40% and 55%. Moving rightward along the income scale, nations such as India, Pakistan, and Guatemala show moderate stunting levels between 30% and 45% with GDP per capita around $5,000 to $10,000. Further along the curve, countries like Egypt, Algeria, and Argentina display stunting rates between 10% and 20% with incomes ranging from $15,000 to $30,000. At the highest income levels, European nations including Belarus and Estonia, alongside Brunei, appear in the bottom-right with stunting rates below 5% and GDP per capita exceeding $40,000. The metric is age-standardized to allow comparisons between countries and over time. GDP per capita is expressed in international dollars at 2021 prices. The distribution forms a downward-sloping curve where increases in GDP per capita correspond to decreases in the share of children who are stunted.
